question.text: During the sowing season of an agricultural commodity, the futures market may show backwardation because:

answer.text: Spot supplies are scarce now but harvesting is expected to increase supply later, so futures price reflects lower expected future prices
Interest rates are high during sowing season
Farmers stop selling in futures during sowing
Government imposes export restrictions during sowing

explanation.text: **The Logic:** During sowing, spot supply is low (scarce) → high spot price. But after 3-4 months harvesting will increase supply → lower expected prices. So futures (reflecting future expectations) trade below spot → backwardation. **The Trap:** Interest rates don't specifically spike during sowing. Farmer activity and export restrictions are not the primary cause. Seasonality of supply is the fundamental driver. **Cross-Exam Context:** Agricultural seasonality causing backwardation is unique to commodity derivatives. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Backwardation during sowing season is predictable — traders can structure calendar spreads to profit from this seasonal pattern in agricultural futures.
